chapter: Chapter 12: Magnetic Effects of Electric Current

questions.question: Define magnetic field.
questions.answer: Magnetic field is the space around a magnet or a current-carrying conductor in which magnetic force is experienced by other magnets or moving charges.

questions.question: Name the instrument used by Oersted to show magnetic effect of current.
questions.answer: Oersted used a magnetic compass needle to demonstrate that electric current produces a magnetic field.

questions.question: What is the shape of magnetic field lines around a straight current-carrying conductor?
questions.answer: The magnetic field lines form concentric circles with the conductor at the centre.

questions.question: State the right-hand thumb rule for determining direction of magnetic field.
questions.answer: If the thumb of right hand points in the direction of current, the direction of curled fingers gives the direction of magnetic field lines.

questions.question: What is a solenoid?
questions.answer: A solenoid is a long coil of many turns of insulated copper wire wound in cylindrical shape.

questions.question: How does the magnetic field inside a solenoid behave?
questions.answer: Inside a long solenoid, the magnetic field is uniform, strong, and parallel to its axis; outside it is very weak.

questions.question: State Fleming's left-hand rule.
questions.answer: If forefinger, middle finger and thumb of left hand are stretched mutually perpendicular: forefinger points magnetic field, middle finger current, thumb force.

questions.question: What is the principle of an electric motor?
questions.answer: Electric motor works on the principle that a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a mechanical force.

questions.question: What is the function of split ring commutator in DC motor?
questions.answer: Split ring commutator reverses the direction of current in the coil after every half rotation to keep the coil rotating in the same direction.

questions.question: Define electromagnetic induction.
questions.answer: Electromagnetic induction is the phenomenon of producing an induced current in a closed circuit due to change in magnetic flux linked with it.

questions.question: State Fleming's right-hand rule.
questions.answer: If forefinger, thumb and middle finger of right hand are stretched mutually perpendicular: forefinger - magnetic field, thumb - motion, middle finger - induced current.

questions.question: What is an electric generator?
questions.answer: Electric generator converts mechanical energy into electrical energy based on electromagnetic induction.

questions.question: Differentiate between AC and DC generator in terms of commutator.
questions.answer: AC generator uses slip rings (produces alternating current), DC generator uses split ring commutator (produces direct current).

questions.question: Why are magnetic field lines closed curves?
questions.answer: Magnetic field lines form closed loops because magnetic monopoles do not exist; lines emerge from north pole and enter south pole, continuing inside the magnet.

questions.question: Why do field lines never intersect each other?
questions.answer: If two field lines intersect, it would mean magnetic field has two directions at the point of intersection, which is impossible.

questions.question: How can the strength of magnetic field of a solenoid be increased?
questions.answer: By increasing current, increasing number of turns per unit length, or inserting soft iron core inside the solenoid.

questions.question: Why is soft iron preferred as core in electromagnets?
questions.answer: Soft iron increases magnetic field strength significantly and loses its magnetism immediately when current is switched off.

questions.question: Explain the role of brushes in an electric motor.
questions.answer: Brushes maintain electrical contact between the split ring commutator and the external circuit, allowing current to flow into the coil continuously.

questions.question: Why do we use parallel circuits in domestic wiring?
questions.answer: Parallel circuits ensure each appliance gets full voltage, can be switched on/off independently, and if one fails, others continue to work.

questions.question: What is the purpose of earth wire in household circuits?
questions.answer: Earth wire provides a low-resistance path for leakage current to flow to ground, preventing electric shock if live wire touches appliance body.

questions.question: Differentiate between short-circuiting and overloading.
questions.answer: Short-circuiting: Live and neutral wires touch directly causing very high current. Overloading: Too many appliances switched on simultaneously exceed safe current limit.

questions.question: How does a fuse protect electrical circuits?
questions.answer: Fuse has low melting point wire; excessive current melts it, breaking the circuit and preventing damage from overheating or fire.

questions.question: Why is induced current produced when a magnet is moved towards a coil?
questions.answer: Movement of magnet changes magnetic flux linked with coil, inducing emf and current according to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction.

questions.question: Give reason why galvanometer needle deflects when current is passed through a nearby wire.
questions.answer: Current in wire produces magnetic field which exerts force on magnetic needle, causing deflection (Oersted's experiment).

questions.question: Explain why the magnetic field is stronger near the poles of a bar magnet.
questions.answer: Field lines are closer together near poles, indicating stronger magnetic field; density of field lines is proportional to field strength.
